title: Preventing fruit flies in a compost bin
subtitle: Managing moisture, cover material, and bin design to deter Drosophila without pesticide
excerpt: Fruit fly infestations in kitchen compost bins and outdoor compost heaps are caused by exposed wet organic material at the correct breeding temperature. The fix is always the same: bury fresh additions under carbon cover material, correct moisture, and prevent access. No chemicals are needed or appropriate.
sourceNotes: RHS guidance on common garden pests: fruit flies. Garden Organic troubleshooting composting guide. University of California IPM (Integrated Pest Management) on Drosophila in compost (US source, principle is universal).

glossaryTerms.definition: The two categories of compost material. Browns are carbon-rich and dry (cardboard, straw, dry leaves). Greens are nitrogen-rich and moist (kitchen scraps, grass, plant trimmings).
The carbon-to-nitrogen ratio of composting material. Browns (high carbon) and greens (high nitrogen) should be balanced at roughly 25:1 to 30:1 for active hot composting.

content.text: Drosophila (fruit flies) lay eggs on exposed moist organic material above 15 degrees Celsius. A kitchen caddies sitting open on the worktop, or a compost bin heap with fresh kitchen waste on the surface, provides exactly the right conditions. The fruit fly life cycle from egg to adult is 7 to 10 days at 25 degrees Celsius: an unmanaged infestation multiplies rapidly. The control strategy is to deny access to exposed moist material at every stage from kitchen caddies to outdoor bin.
Kitchen caddies
Empty the kitchen caddies into the outdoor bin every 2 to 3 days in summer, not weekly. A full caddies held for a week in a warm kitchen is a fruit fly nursery.
Line the caddies with a layer of newspaper or a compostable bag so the base is not directly exposed moist material.
Keep the lid on the kitchen caddies when not in use. A caddies with a tight-fitting lid and an activated carbon filter reduces fruit fly access by 90 percent compared to an open bowl.
Outdoor compost bin
Every addition of
greens
(kitchen waste, fresh plant material) must be covered immediately with at least 50 mm of
browns

: shredded cardboard, dry leaves, or straw. Fruit flies cannot reach eggs through 50 mm of carbon cover, and without exposed surface they do not lay. This one practice eliminates most fly problems without any other intervention.
Bin design and access

A compost bin with a solid lid keeps rain off and reduces fly access. Bins with a loose lid or no lid allow fruit flies, and also allow excess moisture in heavy rain, which encourages anaerobic conditions that attract more flies. In summer, check the base of a plastic compost bin: they often rest directly on soil and allow fly access from below. Placing the bin on a mesh base (hardware cloth, 6 mm mesh) prevents this without disrupting worm access.
If you already have an infestation
Add a thick layer (100 mm) of dry cardboard or straw to the surface of the heap immediately. This buries the breeding surface.
Turn the heap if possible, burying the infested surface layer 300 to 400 mm into the heap. The heat of an active heap kills eggs and larvae at 55 degrees Celsius within 24 hours.
For the indoor kitchen caddies, wash with hot soapy water and allow to dry before relining.
Maintain the cover-material discipline going forward: one part greens to two to three parts browns by volume, every time.

Check for uncovered pockets: material tucked against the bin sides does not get covered when the layer is added to the centre. Ensure cover material is spread across the entire surface, including edges.

In a heated house, the kitchen caddies is breeding flies if left too long. Wintertime outdoor bin flies are unusual and usually indicate a buried pocket of high-nitrogen material that is still warm. Turn and add cardboard.
